coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot soil moisture sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Kalita, Hemen
Palaparthy, Vinay S.
Baghini, Maryam Shojaei
Aslam, M.
coredata.dc:description: A soil sensor to measure moisture content in soil is fabricated and described. The sensor is based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and is highly sensitive. The simplicity of the process and use of an inexpensive material GQDs make it an affordable low cost sensing unit in comparison to existing soil moisture sensing units. It is a resistive micro-sensor with interdigitated electrodes (IDE) where MEMS fabrication process is used and GQDs serve as the channel material between IDEs. The sensor is tested in two different soil environments viz. white clay (passive soil) and bentonite clay (an active soil). The conductance of IDE structure with GQDs changes from 0.06×10−6 1/Ω to 0.68×10−6 1/Ω in white clay as the gravimetric moisture content changes from 4 to 45%. For bentonite soil, the conductance of the sensor changes from 0.06×10−6 1/Ω to 0.48×10−6 1/Ω across the gravimetric moisture range of 11 to 90%. The sensor shows higher sensitivity at higher moisture content which can be attributed to Grotthuss chain reaction and ionic conductivity. Response time of the fabricated micro-sensor is found to be 2–3min which is the lowest response time reported for a resistive based soil moisture sensor. The sensitivity of sensor towards gravimetric moisture contents is 0.014×10−6 1/Ω per percent increase in moisture content (1/Ω/1%) for the white clay and 0.005×10−6 1/Ω/1% for the bentonite soil.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Graphene quantum dots
MEMS
Humidity sensor
Soil moisture sensor
Agriculture application

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: IIT Bombay and Centres of Excellence in Nanoelectronics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Central Scanning Probe Microscope (SPM) facility, the Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science (CRNTS), IIT Bombay and Centres of Excellence in Nanoelectronics, IIT-Bombay. The authors are thankful to Department of Science and Technology (DST) for financial assistance. The authors are thankful for the support received from Prof. D. N. Singh and Sushalekshmi, Environmental Geotechnical lab, IIT-Bombay for providing soil samples and characterizing of soil samples. Hemen Kalita is currently working as a Ph.D. student in the Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay under Prof. M. Aslam. He received his M. Sc. in physics with specialization in Condensed Matter Physics and Electronics from Department of Physics, Gauhati University. He has qualified the qualified the prestigious National eligibility Test (NET) Exam with All India Rank-50. His current research work includes synthesis of graphene, graphene-oxide, graphene quantum dots and studying the transport and sensing properties of graphene based devices and sensors such as FETs, TFTs, humidity sensors, soil moisture sensors and chemical sensors etc. He is also looking into the water purification properties of surface tailored graphene based adsorbents. He has published 7 international journals, 3 patents and 8 conference papers till date. Vinay S. Palaparthy received M.Sc. degree with first rank from Nagpur University, Nagpur, India. He is awarded with INSPIRE Fellowship for pursing Ph.D. degree from Department of Science and Technology (DST). He is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the field of sensor design and embedded system design from Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science (CRNTS), Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai India. He was working as a Research Associate in Indian institute of Technology Bombay before joining for doctoral degree. He has 3 conference papers and 2 international journals on his ongoing work. He has one best paper award in international journal. Maryam Shojaei Baghini received M.S. and Ph.D. degree in Electrical Engineering from Sharif University of Technology, in 1991 and 1999, respectively. She worked in industry from 1991 to 1992 and 1999–2000 as senior analog IC design engineer. In 2001 she joined Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT-Bombay) as a post-doctoral fellow, where she is currently a Professor in Department of Electrical Engineering. Dr. Maryam Shojaei Baghini is author/co-author of more than 135 international journal and conference papers, inventor/co-inventor of 6 issued US patents, one issued Indian patent, and inventor/co-inventor of 20 more patent applications. Mohammed Aslam is an Associate Professor of Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT Bombay). Prior to joining IIT Bombay in January 2008, Aslam was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Materials Science and Engineering Department of Northwestern University. Dr. M. Aslam received a M.Sc. in Physics with specialization in Materials Science from Pune University, in 1996 and a PhD in Physics from the National Chemical Laboratory affiliated to Pune University in 2004. His expertise is in designing materials and particles at the nanoscale (metal and functional oxides) in order to design new electronic, optoelectronic and nanodevice application. Dr. M. Aslam is author/co-author of more than 106 international journal and conference papers having more than 2700 citations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication steps (a)–(f) for interdigitated electrodes (IDE). (g) Equivalent circuit for the IDE structure with the sample as the channel material.
(a) SEM image of IDE with dimensions of 1500μm×2400μm. (b) Sensor packaging with and without filter cap with dimension of 25mm in diameter and 10mm in height.
Experiment set up for the soil moisture sensing measurement. Nitrogen gas (N2) is used to achieve the baseline and the LCR meter is used for resistance measurement.

(a) TEM images of as-prepared graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The top inset shows the size distribution of GQDs. Average diameter of the dots is calculated to be 4.5±0.55nm. The bottom Inset shows high resolution crystalline image of single GQD particle. (b) AFM image of the dots deposited on a mica substrate and the corresponding sectional analysis shows the average height of the dots to be 1–2nm. (c) The Raman spectra of as-prepared GQDs with a G band at 1590cm−1, D band at 1350cm−1.
(a) Wide region XPS survey spectrum and (b) Deconvoluted C1s spectrum of GQDs.
(a) Variation of the electrical resistance of the GQDs based soil sensor for (a) white clay and (b) bentonite clay soil with different gravimetric moisture content as specified in the graph.
Three cycle response of developed micro-sensor on (a) 12% gravimetric moisture content of white clay soil. (a) 11% gravimetric moisture content of bentonite clay soil.
Conductance vs gravimetric moisture content plot for the bentonite and white clay soil.

(a) The variation of the resistance of the GQDs micro-sensor with time. (b) Change in resistance of the micro sensor with increase in temperature at ambience relative humidity (RH). ΔR=R0 −Rt, where R0 is resistance of the micro sensor measured at 25°C temperature and Rt is resistance of the sensor measured at temperature ‘t’.

An illustration of the soil moisture sensing mechanism showing (a) the first physisorbed layer (i) of the water molecule (b) the second physisorbed layer (ii) of the water molecules. The sensing is explained by the interaction of the actives sites of the GQDs with the water molecules via hydrogen bonding.
Peak position and integrated intensity values (%) of the deconvoluted components of the C1s core level of as prepared GQDs.
Comparison of different paramaters of the proposed GQD soil moisture micro-sensor with those reported in the literature.

row.entry: 0.014×10−6 1/Ω/1% for the white clay and 0.005×10−6 1/Ω/1% for the bentonite soil
2.5 to 4.0M ohm for 15–35% change in gravimetric water content
43.6, 275 and 78.6μV/0.1% for bentonite soil, white clay and sand, respectively

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot soil moisture sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: First two authors have equal contribution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A soil sensor to measure moisture content in soil is fabricated and described. The sensor is based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and is highly sensitive. The simplicity of the process and use of an inexpensive material GQDs make it an affordable low cost sensing unit in comparison to existing soil moisture sensing units. It is a resistive micro-sensor with interdigitated electrodes (IDE) where MEMS fabrication process is used and GQDs serve as the channel material between IDEs. The sensor is tested in two different soil environments viz. white clay (passive soil) and bentonite clay (an active soil). The conductance of IDE structure with GQDs changes from 0.06×10−6 1/Ω to 0.68×10−6 1/Ω in white clay as the gravimetric moisture content changes from 4 to 45%. For bentonite soil, the conductance of the sensor changes from 0.06×10−6 1/Ω to 0.48×10−6 1/Ω across the gravimetric moisture range of 11 to 90%. The sensor shows higher sensitivity at higher moisture content which can be attributed to Grotthuss chain reaction and ionic conductivity. Response time of the fabricated micro-sensor is found to be 2–3min which is the lowest response time reported for a resistive based soil moisture sensor. The sensitivity of sensor towards gravimetric moisture contents is 0.014×10−6 1/Ω per percent increase in moisture content (1/Ω/1%) for the white clay and 0.005×10−6 1/Ω/1% for the bentonite soil.

ce:section.ce:para: In the field of agriculture, continuous monitoring of soil moisture is extremely essential for a proper yield of crops, as soil moisture determines the amount of water present in the soil matrix. Various soil moisture sensors have been reported by the researchers [1–6]. Soil moisture content is measured in two ways viz. gravimetric moisture content (w) and volumetric moisture content (θ). Volumetric soil moisture measurement holds an edge over gravimetric moisture measurements since gravimetric measurements are limited to the lab and cannot be used for in-situ measurements. Time domain reflectometry (TDR), frequency domain reflectometry (FDR) and capacitance probe are some of the techniques used for accurate volumetric moisture measurements [1,3]. However the main drawback of these sensors is that they are expensive and cannot be afforded by many farmers [1,3]. Another reliable and low cost solution is dual probe heat pulse sensor but high power consumption is a major flaw in such system [1,3,7,8]. Researchers have also focused on polymer based soil moisture sensor using MEMS devices, where the resistance changes with increase in the soil moisture content [9,10]. Besides that, cantilever based soil moisture sensor was also reported recently, where, polyaniline a moisture sensitive material is used on top of the cantilever [11]. Polyaniline is a piezoresistive polymer which reacts with water molecules present in the soil thereby creating stress on the cantilever beam which causes the cantilever beam to deflect [11]. Similarly, cantilever based soil moisture sensor having water sensitive nano-polymer film on top of the cantilever beam has also been reported [12]. The drawback of the cantilever based sensor is the instability apart from the fact that fabricating cantilevers is an expensive process as well. Single step lithography for fabricating the IDE structure is a cost effective alternative as compared to the cantilever based sensors. In comparison to simple two-probe electrode, IDE will increase the effective contact area of the channel material and thus increase the sensitivity as well. Researchers have also reported electrode based micro-sensor which uses PEDOT:PSS conducting polymer [13]. When exposed to the moisture, conductivity of PEDOT:PSS changes due to the change in the resistance [13]. The disadvantage of resistive based technique is its high response time (2–3h) and short life span [1,3]. The electrodes used in these sensors get oxidized, hence lifespan of these sensors is short [1]. Therefore, use of gold (Au) as the electrode enables us to achieve good stability and higher life time due to its low oxidation (corrosion resistant) nature. Thus, soil moisture sensor with a wider range of moisture detection, high sensitivity, long life, quicker response time and shorter recovery time are essential to design a state of the art sensor. In order to meet these basic requirements with greater efficiency, researchers are focusing on development of new moisture sensitive materials. Nanomaterials such as 1-D carbon nanotubes, and graphene offer an attractive option as material of choice for soil moisture sensing due to their high surface to volume ratio in comparison to the polymers and metal oxides [14,15].

Out of these many new materials, graphene has been at the centre stage of material research since its discovery [16–19]. Graphene is 2-dimensional single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice structure and has shown tremendous potential for various sensing applications, mainly due to its unique electrical properties such as low noise level and high carrier mobility and large specific surface area for molecular adsorption [16–19]. Recently, researchers have been exploring sensors and devices based on different forms of graphene for humidity measurements. However, because of its zero band gap semi-metallic nature, graphene has limited use in the field of electronics [18]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the zero-dimensional (0D) version of graphene are being explored as a solution to this shortcoming since GQDs have a finite band gap due to quantum confinement and edge effects [18,20]. GQDs have already been investigated for FETs [21], photovoltaics [22,23], light emitting diodes [24,25], electrochemiluminescence [26], bioimaging [27,28], and biolabelling [29,30]. GQDs can be synthesized by various top-down [31–36] and bottom-up approaches [37–39]. In addition, our research group has recently shown that electrochemically synthesized GQDs are highly sensitive to humidity [40]. Taking the advantage of the quantum size of the GQDs, smaller size devices (channel width few nm) would be feasible in future leading to a NEMS device formulation which could be more suitable option for the future IC technology and sensors. Thus, IDE based micro-sensor with GQDs as sensing material and Au as electrodes can be a suitable candidate for soil moisture sensing.

In this work, resistive-type soil moisture sensor is fabricated with electrochemically synthesized graphene quantum dots as the sensing material. Cr/Au inter-digitated electrode (IDE) deposited on Si/SiO2 substrate is used to fabricate the micro-sensor. The sensor has a short response time of 2–3min for a wide range of soil moisture. The sensor is stable for about 4 months with negligible change in resistance. The sensitivity of the sensor towards gravimetric moisture contents is 0.014×10−6 1/Ω per percentage increase in moisture (1/Ω/1%) for the white clay and 0.005×10−6 1/Ω per percentage increase in moisture (1/Ω/1%) for the bentonite soil. The soil sensing mechanism is explained using Grotthuss chain reaction and ionic conductivity.

Micro-sensor was fabricated on Si wafer having interdigitated electrodes (IDE) structure using MEMS fabrication process. IDE structure offers a high sensitivity and increases the volume for sample deposit as compared to the electrodes [13]. We have used gold (Au) as electrodes, which results in good stability and higher life span due to its low oxidation nature. On top of the IDE, 20μL of electrochemically synthesized graphene quantum dots dispersed in water (0.1mg/mL) were dropcasted and air dried for 3–4h. In order to protect the micro-sensors from damage, packaging is a crucial parameter for agricultural applications [11,13]. We have used a sensiron (SHT1X) filter cap on the top of IDE electrodes which allows only soil moisture to diffuse inside the chamber and interact with IDE micro-sensor. Such packaging does not allow the soil particles to penetrate inside the chamber and protect the micro sensor from damage [41]. Fabricated micro-sensor is tested in two different soil types’ viz. white clay soil and bentonite clay soil. Soil sample is prepared with different moisture contents and its moisture is measured by infrared balance moisture which determines the gravimetric moisture content (Eq. (1)). Detailed synthesis of GQDs, fabrication of IDE structured micro-sensor and packaging is explained in the following subsections.
(1)
G r a v i m e t r i c m o i s t u r e c o n t e n t
( w )
=
W e t w e i g h t o f s o i l − D r y w e i g h t o f s o i l
D r y w e i g h t o f s o i l

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs used for our study are electrochemically synthesized using the method which has been discussed in our previous work [40]. In a typical synthesis about 5μL of multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) (0.5mg/mL) dispersed in ethanol was deposited onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) using a micro tip. Prior to deposition of MWCNT, GCE is polished in alumina powder and rinsed/washed thoroughly with acetone and water, and then dried subsequently. The MWCNT coated GCE is kept under an IR lamp for about 30min to remove the water content and MWCNT forms a thin uniform layer over it. The MWCNT is electrochemically oxidized at a fixed potential of 1.1V in an Autolab system with the three-electrode system (GCE as working electrode, Pt as counter electrode and Pt wire as the reference electrode). Propylene carbonate containing 3mM LiClO4 is used as the supporting electrolyte.

In the positive potential of 1V, breaking of sp2 carbon atoms start due to the applied electric field in the working electrode i.e. GCE. After oxidation, the potential was fixed at −1V. In the negative potential, the oxidized MWCNTs get exfoliated because of the intercalation of Li+ ion complex. The exfoliations of oxidized MWCNTs results in size-tunable GQDs. Samples are collected by sonicating the GCE electrode in deionized water. Samples are washed and dialyzed before doing any characterization [40].

Fabrication of IDE involves single step lithography and hence low cost sensors can be fabricated as compared to cantilevers which have minimum 2–5 layer lithographic processes [11,42]. The IDE also offers an advantage of having high sensitivity and an increase in the volume of sample deposit. Fabrication steps for IDEs are shown in Fig. 1 .

Initially, p-type wafer with <100> orientation is cleaned using standard RCA cleaning procedure. After RCA cleaning 1μm oxide is grown using thermal oxidation as shown in Fig. 1(a). Positive photoresist (PPR) S1813 is spin coated on the wafer at 3000rpm by using spin coater and pre bake it for 3min at 90°C as shown in Fig. 1(b). A mask was prepared for IDE to pattern it on the Si wafer with dimensions (1500μm×2400μm). The PPR is exposed to UV light by using Karl Suss MJB-3 mask aligner, followed by a post bake for 2min at 90°C, and development of pattern in MF319 solution for 30s, and then rinsing with isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and drying using N2 gas. Accordingly, we get a pattern as shown in Fig. 1(c). Fig. 1(d) shows deposition of Cr/Au (20nm/200nm) on SiO2 by the metal sputter process. 20nm chrome (Cr) is usually deposited for the purpose of good adhesion between the SiO2 and gold (Au). PPR is removed along with Cr/Au present on the top of PPR by using the lift off technique shown in Fig. 1(e). In the lift off technique we keep the wafer in acetone followed by an ultrasonic bath for 5–10min. The wafer is then cleaned by using IPA followed by rinsing with distilled water (DI). As shown in Fig. 1(f) 20μL of electrochemically synthesized GQDs dispersed in water (0.1mg/mL) are dropcasted in between IDE electrodes and air dried for 3–4h. In our previous work, study of response of the GQDs was limited to humidity measurement [40], whereas in our proposed work we have explored the use of GQDs for soil moisture sensing application. The conventional humidity sensor [40], detects water molecules present in the ambient atmosphere, but other air contaminations present in the atmosphere might also play some role in the sensing mechanism. On the other hand, the soil moisture sensor detects the water molecules released from the soil which pass through the protective filter cap on the micro-sensor. The design of the soil moisture sensor is advantageous because it will allow only water molecules to get inside the chamber and block the soil particle outside. This kind of arrangement (sealed filter cap) will protect the sensor from damage, thus increasing the lifetime of the sensor. It will remove the chance of false signal through other entities i.e. in this design; the sealed cap arrangement protects the sensor thus making it less prone to contamination from the ambient environment. In addition, the soil moisture sensor is designed and fabricated as a commercial template of micro-sensor (micron range dimension) suitable for MEMS technology and can be tested directly in field, whereas the earlier report [40] is a simple 2-probe device and it is only lab-scale device. In the earlier report [40], 2-probe electrode method was used and GQDs were drop casted on it while in the present work, we have used IDE which increases the surface area for the sample deposition and sensitivity. Fig. 2 (a) shows an SEM image of the fabricated IDE structure on Si substrate. Fig. 2(b) shows a sensor placed beneath the filter cap with proper encapsulation.

White clay soil and bentonite soil were selected for carrying out the experimental measurements. First, the soil samples were completely dried and this was achieved by keeping the soil samples inside the oven at 105°C for 24h. The moisture present in the soil sample will be completely evaporated during these 24h. The complete dry condition of the soil was confirmed by infrared balance moisture instruments which determine the gravimetric moisture content (w). Soil samples were prepared with different moisture contents and taken in a perspex mould. Moisture content levels were verified by an infrared moisture balance instrument which measures the gravimetric moisture content. The soil moisture sensor was tested over a wide range of moisture content (from 4 to 90%). The value of the applied gravimetric moisture range was within the practical range of the moisture in the soil samples in the field. This range generally depends on the water holding capacity of the soil samples. The liquid limit is the state of the soil in which soil will become slurry. White clay soil, which was used in the presented work, has the liquid limit of around 55–60%.The maximum gravimetric moisture content was generally selected at a value below the liquid limit. Before subjecting to moisture, we purged the sensor with nitrogen gas (N2) to remove any water molecules present on the top of the electrode. When the sensor was purged with nitrogen gas (N2), it went back to its initial state where the sensor was not exposed to water molecules; this initial state serves as a baseline. In previously proposed methods/devices, the device has no provision for nitrogen purging (for baseline correction) within the sensor design. Hence we have improvised upon the overall design to include on-site provision of nitrogen purging. Arguably, the provision and necessity of nitrogen purging does lead to marginal increase in cost as well as complexity of the micro-sensor. Fig. 3 shows the diagram of the experimental set-up comprising the soil sample kept in the mould, the sensor deployed in the soil and N2 cylinder connected to the soil sensor chamber which allows the sensor to achieve the baseline. Each time, before starting the experiment the sensor is purged with nitrogen gas (N2) to remove the water molecules present in the sensor. A sensor enclosed in a filter cap was deployed in the soil kept in the mould. Agilent LCR meter E4980A is used to measure the change in the resistance of the sensor. The electrical measurements are carried out at room temperature (25°C).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of electrochemically prepared graphene quantum dots is shown in Fig. 4 (a). The prepared dots are confirmed to be monodispersed using transmission electron microscopy and their average diameter is found to be 4.5±0.55nm. The statistical analysis of the GQD sample is done by counting 40–50 particles and using the lognormal distribution. The inset of Fig. 4(a) shows the high crystalline image of the single GQD particle. Fig. 4(b) shows the height distribution of the prepared quantum dots taken using an atomic force microscope. The sample is prepared by drop-casting the as-prepared GQDs solution on the mica substrate and subsequently drying it at ambient conditions. The height of the as-prepared quantum dots is found to be around 1–2nm, which corresponds to one to two layers of graphene [32]. In Raman spectroscopy of carbon nanostructures, the G band is assigned to the E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms, and the D band corresponds to the extent of defects [43]. Fig. 4(c) shows the Raman spectrum of the as-prepared dots with a G band at 1590cm−1, D band at 1350cm−1. The Raman spectrum confirms the presence of defects in case of the as-prepared GQDs.

All the electrical measurements are carried out at room temperature (25°C). In order to eliminate the effect of the polarization effect caused from the physisorbed layer we performed alternating current (AC) measurements at 1kHz and excited with 1V AC. Voltage was kept below 1.5V in order to avoid electrolysis of water molecules. The measurements corresponding to the baseline are recorded after the introduction of N2 in the test chamber. For every moisture level we set the baseline by N2 purging. Fig. 6 shows the change in resistance for bentonite and white clay having different gravimetric moisture value. In Fig. 6 (a) at 1V, the resistance offered by the GQDs based soil sensor is determined to be 15MΩ, 4.85MΩ, 2.64MΩ and 1.47MΩ, corresponding to 4, 15, 30 and 45% (standard deviation, σ<1) respectively for white clay. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 6(b), the resistance offered by the GQDs based soil sensor is determined to be 15.5MΩ, 4.85MΩ, 3.3MΩ and 2.05MΩ, corresponding to 11, 30, 60 and 90% (standard deviation, σ<1) respectively for bentonite soil. From Fig. 6, it is observed that resistance decreases with an increase in soil gravimetric moisture content. This response is due to the adsorption of water molecules coming from the soil surface onto the graphene quantum dots surface. Increasing the soil moisture level leads to higher adsorption of water molecules and strengthening the coupling between dots, which increases the sensitivity. When N2 is introduced, the resistance value for an applied voltage increases, which is due to the removal of the adsorbed water molecules from the surface of the GQDs. Water is known to enhance the conductivity in pristine graphene [19], as doping of graphene with water substantially increases the concentration of holes and hence, renders it as p-type [18]. Therefore, an increase in adsorption of water would lead to an increase in hole doping, which in turn will enhance the hole transport in the quantum dot system. The white clay and bentonite soil exhibits different properties such as liquid limit, plastic limit, ionic content etc [11,46]. White clay is considered as the passive soil where as bentonite is considered as the active soil due their mineral content [46]. All these factors may lead to difference in sensitivity which is in fact seen in our experiments. The difference in sensitivity of sensor towards white clay and the bentonite soil observed in our experimental results may be due to a different water retention limit for the respective soil type but the exact reason in still unknown [11].

The response time of the micro-sensor on white clay and bentonite clay soil was also studied with 12% and 11% gravimetric moisture content at a fixed AC voltage of 1V at 1kHz. In this way the polarization effect is eliminated, which originates from the physisorbed water. Similar procedure has been previously reported [47,48]. In this experiment, first the micro-sensor is deployed in the soil and then N2 gas is purged to achieve the baseline value. The same process is repeated for both the soil response measurements. When sensor is exposed to soil moisture (ON state), the resistance initially decreases and thereafter becomes stable after∼180s as seen in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). N2 was purged in an interval of 200s for white clay and 150s for bentonite soil, respectively, to achieve the baseline value (OFF state). Fig. 8 (a) and (b) show a cyclic response of the sensor for bentonite clay and white clay for three cycles respectively.

The sensor’s response time is very fast and is approx 2–3min (Note: The response time is considered as fast response time for a resistive sensor). The baseline value is achieved each time when we purge sensor with N2 gas which indicates that the water molecules are playing the main role in the conductivity. The baseline is almost a constant for both bentonite clay and white clay soil. The time required to achieve the baseline after the purging of the N2 gas is the recovery time and it is around 4–5s. Fig. 8 shows a relation between conductance offered by the sensor for different gravimetric moisture contents for both bentonite and white clay soil. The conductance for the white clay is found to be more as compared to the bentonite soil. The slope of the fitted line gives the rate of change of conductance with gravimetric moisture content i.e. sensitivity which is calculated using Eq. (2). Sensitivity is defined as the rate of change of conductance with gravimetric moisture content, given by the equation:
(2)
S e n s i t i v i t y
( S )
=
c
2
−
c
1
g
2
−
g
1
where, c1 and c2 are the conductance (1/Ω) value at an initial and final point and g1 and g2 are the corresponding gravimetric moisture value (%).

The sensitivity of the sensor towards gravimetric moisture contents was calculated to be 0.014×10−6 1/Ω per percentage increase in moisture (1/Ω/1%) for the white clay and 0.005×10−6 1/Ω per percentage increase in moisture (1/Ω/1%) for the bentonite soil.

The micro-sensor is tested over a period of 4 months as shown in Fig. 9 (a). The resistance of the sensor is found to be constant over that period of time confirming the stability of the GQDs micro-sensor. The comparison of different parameters of the proposed GQDs micro-sensor is tabulated with respect to earlier reported micro-sensor as shown in Table 2 . From Table 2 it can be seen that our fabricated sensor is more stable and having shorter response time. We have also studied the temperature dependence of resistance of the micro-sensor at ambience condition as shown in Fig. 9b. The ambience relative humidity value was fixed at 53%, which was measured at room temperature (25°C). The sensor was heated from 25°C to 60°C and the resistance was measured at 1V and the resistance was found to increase with increase in temperature. The increase in resistance can be explained by the removal of water molecules adsorbed on the GQDs surface with the increase in temperature. As a future scope, an upgradation is required to eliminate the effect of the temperature on the response of the sensor.

The possible mechanism for the moisture sensing by the array of the GQDs channel material in the IDE structure is shown in Fig. 10 . The active sites of GQDs (encircled functional groups at the surfaces (Fig. 10a) are the different functional groups such as epoxy, carboxyl, hydroxyl present on the surface of the GQDs. At low moisture content, water molecules are mainly physisorbed onto the available active sites (oxygen functional groups) of the GQD surface through double hydrogen bonding, which is labeled as the first-layer physisorption of water (Fig. 10a). As the moisture level increases, the multilayer physical adsorption of water molecules occurs. The water molecules in the second physisorbed layer (Fig. 10b), are attached to the first physisorbed layer through single hydrogen bonding on the hydroxyl groups. In the first physisorption layer, the motions of the water molecules are not free because of the constraint from double hydrogen bonding. The GQDs film shows high electrical resistance as the hopping transfer of protons between adjacent hydroxyl groups in the first-layer requires higher energy. In the second physisorption regime, the water molecules are similar to those in the bulk liquid as the molecules are free to move. The liquid-like behavior is gradually observed in the physisorbed water layers with the increase in moisture level. Under the influence of an applied electric field, the water in the higher physisorbed regime can be ionized to hydronium ions (H3O+) which can act as a charge carrier. In bulk like liquid, an efficient proton hopping between adjacent water molecules occurs at GQD surface via the Grotthuss chain reaction (H2O+H3O+ →H3O+ +H2O) [14,49]. The GQDs are functionalized with high concentration of oxygen functional groups as confirmed by the XPS analysis which leads to increase in interaction resulting in increase in sensitivity. Additionally, at higher moisture value, the physisorbed water can penetrate into the GQD film network, which is favorable for the hydrolysis of the functional groups (carboxyl, and/or hydroxyl) on GQDs, and these ions can contribute to the ionic conductivity [14]. All these factors lead to high value of current at higher moisture levels as shown by the experimental results.

In summary, we have reported electrochemically synthesized GQDs based soil moisture sensor. The soil moisture sensor is responsive over a wide range of moisture content (from 4% to 90%) for both bentonite and white clay soils. The sensor is stable for about 4 months (and still counting) and there is very negligible change in the resistance with time. The response time of the sensor is around 2–3min which is quite fast for agriculture related applications. The physisorption of H2O molecules (via hydrogen bonding) with the active sites of the GQDs is responsible for sensing characteristics. The Grotthuss chain reaction and ionic conductivity are the possible phenomena responsible for high sensitivity at high moisture content. The result herein demonstrates the potential application of GQDs for fast and accurate measurements of soil moisture in the field of agriculture technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Central Scanning Probe Microscope (SPM) facility, the Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science (CRNTS), IIT Bombay and Centres of Excellence in Nanoelectronics, IIT-Bombay. The authors are thankful to Department of Science and Technology (DST) for financial assistance. The authors are thankful for the support received from Prof. D. N. Singh and Sushalekshmi, Environmental Geotechnical lab, IIT-Bombay for providing soil samples and characterizing of soil samples.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hemen Kalita is currently working as a Ph.D. student in the Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay under Prof. M. Aslam. He received his M. Sc. in physics with specialization in Condensed Matter Physics and Electronics from Department of Physics, Gauhati University. He has qualified the qualified the prestigious National eligibility Test (NET) Exam with All India Rank-50. His current research work includes synthesis of graphene, graphene-oxide, graphene quantum dots and studying the transport and sensing properties of graphene based devices and sensors such as FETs, TFTs, humidity sensors, soil moisture sensors and chemical sensors etc. He is also looking into the water purification properties of surface tailored graphene based adsorbents. He has published 7 international journals, 3 patents and 8 conference papers till date.

Vinay S. Palaparthy received M.Sc. degree with first rank from Nagpur University, Nagpur, India. He is awarded with INSPIRE Fellowship for pursing Ph.D. degree from Department of Science and Technology (DST). He is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree in the field of sensor design and embedded system design from Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Science (CRNTS), Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Mumbai India. He was working as a Research Associate in Indian institute of Technology Bombay before joining for doctoral degree. He has 3 conference papers and 2 international journals on his ongoing work. He has one best paper award in international journal.

Maryam Shojaei Baghini received M.S. and Ph.D. degree in Electrical Engineering from Sharif University of Technology, in 1991 and 1999, respectively. She worked in industry from 1991 to 1992 and 1999–2000 as senior analog IC design engineer. In 2001 she joined Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT-Bombay) as a post-doctoral fellow, where she is currently a Professor in Department of Electrical Engineering. Dr. Maryam Shojaei Baghini is author/co-author of more than 135 international journal and conference papers, inventor/co-inventor of 6 issued US patents, one issued Indian patent, and inventor/co-inventor of 20 more patent applications.

Mohammed Aslam is an Associate Professor of Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT Bombay). Prior to joining IIT Bombay in January 2008, Aslam was a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Materials Science and Engineering Department of Northwestern University. Dr. M. Aslam received a M.Sc. in Physics with specialization in Materials Science from Pune University, in 1996 and a PhD in Physics from the National Chemical Laboratory affiliated to Pune University in 2004. His expertise is in designing materials and particles at the nanoscale (metal and functional oxides) in order to design new electronic, optoelectronic and nanodevice application. Dr. M. Aslam is author/co-author of more than 106 international journal and conference papers having more than 2700 citations.
